ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for braised beef and tripe noodles; mapped to the broader 'noodle dish' class (FOODON_00002473) as the closest defensible ancestor.

ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (composite cooked noodle dish) used as the closest defensible category; no exact code exists for braised beef and tripe noodles in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of braised beef and tripe noodles weighs approximately 600 g, comprising noodles, braised beef and tripe pieces, broth, and garnishes.

nutrient_source.title: Health Promotion Board Singapore — Nutrient Composition of Food (HPB Foodo / Singapore Food Composition Database)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB entry exists for braised beef and tripe noodles as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entries for braised beef (brisket/shank), beef tripe (cooked), wheat noodles (cooked), and beef broth, combined in proportions consistent with a standard 600 g bowl (approximately 30% noodles, 25% beef and tripe, 40% broth, 5% vegetables and garnish). Input per_100g values from the source record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are retained as the working estimate and cross-checked against HPB component data. Values are consistent with HPB data for similar composite noodle dishes. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked beef brisket (NDB 13325) and beef tripe (NDB 13321) used as secondary cross-reference for protein, fat, and cholesterol.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database — wheat noodles (cooked, Chinese-style)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value exists specifically for braised beef and tripe noodles as a composite dish. GI estimated at 52 based on the dominant carbohydrate source: cooked Chinese wheat noodles (egg noodles / yellow noodles), for which the University of Sydney GI Database reports GI values in the range 46–58 (mean ~52, low GI category). Beef, tripe, and broth contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially affect GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 64.8) / 100 = 33.7, rounded to 33.8. Carbohydrate per serving = 10.8 g/100g × 600 g = 64.8 g. GL of 33.8 is high per serving due to the large 600 g bowl size.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce, dark soy sauce, and concentrated beef broth
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 300 mg/100 g yields approximately 1800 mg per 600 g bowl, approaching or exceeding the 2000 mg/day recommended limit in a single meal; individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce, low-sodium broth, and avoid additional condiments such as chilli oil.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load per serving (GL ~34) from refined wheat noodles with very low dietary fibre (0.7 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The large carbohydrate load of approximately 65 g per bowl from refined wheat noodles, combined with dietary fibre well below the 3 g/100 g threshold, produces a high glycemic load of approximately 34 per serving; individuals with type 2 diabetes should substitute wheat noodles with konjac, soba, or wholemeal noodles and reduce portion size.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from beef brisket and tripe, elevated sodium, and cholesterol contribution
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Saturated fat at 1.7 g/100 g (approximately 10 g per bowl) and cholesterol at 20 mg/100 g (120 mg per bowl), combined with high sodium, are relevant for cardiovascular risk; individuals with CVD should limit portion size, choose leaner cuts, and request reduced-sodium broth.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High arachidonic acid (omega-6) load from multiple red meat components (beef brisket, shank, tripe) amplifying pro-inflammatory cytokine pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Beef brisket and tripe are rich in arachidonic acid which amplifies TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory pathways in rheumatoid arthritis; substituting beef and tripe with tofu, tempeh, or seafood while retaining the anti-inflammatory braising spices (ginger, cinnamon, star anise) is recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Braised Beef and Tripe Noodles (红烧牛肉牛杂面) is a hearty Chinese noodle dish popular in Singapore's Chinese hawker and restaurant culture. The dish features slow-braised beef brisket and/or shank alongside honeycomb beef tripe, cooked in a deeply aromatic broth seasoned with soy sauce, dark soy sauce, star anise, cinnamon, ginger, garlic, and Sichuan peppercorns. The braising process renders the beef tender and gelatinous while infusing the tripe with savoury, spiced flavour. The dish is served over springy wheat noodles (typically yellow egg noodles or flat rice noodles) in a generous portion of the braising broth, garnished with spring onions, blanched vegetables, and sometimes sliced radish or chilli oil. Nutritionally, a standard 600 g bowl provides approximately 650 kcal, 45 g protein, 25 g fat, and 65 g carbohydrate. Sodium content is high at approximately 1800 mg per bowl due to soy sauce and concentrated broth. Dietary fibre is low at 0.7 g/100 g. The glycemic load per serving is high (~34) owing to the large portion of refined wheat noodles. Clinically, this dish is unsuitable for individuals with hypertension (high sodium), type 2 diabetes (high glycemic load, low fibre), and rheumatoid arthritis (high arachidonic acid from red meat). It is moderately concerning for cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat and sodium. The dish is not vegetarian, not vegan, and not certified halal due to the use of beef offal. It is dairy-free. Common allergens include gluten, wheat, and soy.

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exists for braised beef and tripe noodles as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database component entries (beef brisket braised, beef tripe cooked, wheat noodles cooked, beef broth) and cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. The input record was ai_generated with confidence 0.7; values are consistent with HPB component data and are retained as a medium-confidence estimate. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked Chinese wheat noodles as the dominant carbohydrate source.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite braised noodle dish is not available in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as a single composite entry. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this composite dish is not reported in the HPB database for this preparation; estimated ~200 mg/100g from radish and spring onions but not confirmed by an approved source and therefore set to -1. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish is not reported in the HPB database. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient data for this composite dish is not available as a single entry in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. Duplicate portion entry in input (two identical '1 bowl / 600 g' entries) — deduplicated to a single serving of 600 g. Halal status is not confirmed; beef and tripe from non-halal-certified sources are common in Chinese hawker settings. Trans fat at 0.08 g/100g is a trace level consistent with naturally occurring trans fats in ruminant meat and is not from industrial hydrogenation.
